title: SpaNorm: spatial-aware normalization
section: Build the SpaNorm assay (one fit per sample, sample.p = 0.25)
text:
Build the SpaNorm assay (one fit per sample, sample.p = 0.25)


Code
# eval:false because this is the one-off build; every render reloads the checkpoint above.

# Sample, slide, and condition lookup; matches R/20260512_xenium2seurat.R for self-containment.
samples <- tribble(
  ~sample_id, ~slide,            ~condition,
  "4s2_F1",   "Slide1_0022474",  "control",
  "4s2M_F1",  "Slide1_0022474",  "switched",
  "4s2M_F2",  "Slide1_0022474",  "switched",
  "4s2_F2",   "Slide2_0069080",  "control",
  "4s2_F3",   "Slide2_0069080",  "control",
  "4s2M_F3",  "Slide2_0069080",  "switched",
  "4s2M_F4",  "Slide2_0069080",  "switched")

# Load the raw merged object; SpaNorm needs raw counts, not the SCT residuals.
xen <- qs_read("data/20260512_microglia_switch_original_seurat.qs2")
DefaultAssay(xen) <- "Xenium"

# Build the sample_id -> FOV-name lookup from the object itself; LoadXenium uses default name "fov" and merge renames collisions
# (so the actual names are usually fov / fov.2, not the slide labels).
fov_to_slide <- map_chr(Images(xen), \(img) {
  # Each FOV's cells share the same slide value in meta.data; use the first one as the slide tag.
  unique(xen@meta.data[Cells(xen[[img]]), "slide"])[1]
}) |> set_names(Images(xen))
# Invert: slide -> FOV, then index by sample slide to get the per-sample FOV lookup.
slide_to_fov <- set_names(names(fov_to_slide), unname(fov_to_slide))
fov_lookup <- set_names(slide_to_fov[samples$slide], samples$sample_id)

# Run SpaNorm on one sample at the package-default subsample. Returns the log-scale normalized matrix.
spanorm_one_sample <- function(s, xen, fov_lookup) {
  # Subset keeps only the cells of one tissue section; SpaNorm's smoothness assumption is per section.
  seu_s <- subset(xen, subset = sample_id == s)
  # Centroids come from the slide FOV; we filter to the cells that survived the per-sample subset.
  coords <- GetTissueCoordinates(seu_s, image = fov_lookup[[s]]) |>
    filter(cell %in% Cells(seu_s))
  # SpatialExperiment is SpaNorm's native input; we hand it raw counts plus the 2D centroids.
  spe <- SpatialExperiment(
    assays        = list(counts = GetAssayData(seu_s, assay = "Xenium", layer = "counts")),
    spatialCoords = as.matrix(coords[, c("x", "y")]))
  # sample.p=0.25 is the package default; the only change from 20260512_SpaNorm.qmd.
  spe <- SpaNorm(spe,
                 sample.p   = 0.25,
                 df.tps     = 6,
                 adj.method = "auto",
                 backend    = "auto",
                 verbose    = TRUE)
  # logcounts holds the normalized matrix; we keep Seurat's cell barcodes for the later cbind.
  out <- SummarizedExperiment::assay(spe, "logcounts")
  colnames(out) <- Cells(seu_s)
  out}

# Loop over the 7 samples; map keeps the per-sample matrices named for the reduce step.
norm_list <- map(samples$sample_id, spanorm_one_sample,
                 xen = xen, fov_lookup = fov_lookup) |>
  set_names(samples$sample_id)

#qd_save(norm_list, "data/20260513_norm_list_sample025.qs2")

# Bind the per-sample matrices into one wide normalized matrix and reorder to the merged object's cell order.
# Qualify reduce() because SpatialExperiment loads IRanges, whose reduce() masks purrr::reduce.
norm_mat <- purrr::reduce(norm_list, cbind)
norm_mat <- norm_mat[, Cells(xen)]

# Attach as a new v5 assay; data layer only because the auto-selected adjustment returns log-scale residuals.
# CreateAssay5Object avoids the "counts layer not present" warning that CreateAssayObject (v3) triggers inside a v5 object.
xen[["SpaNorm"]] <- CreateAssay5Object(data = norm_mat)
DefaultAssay(xen) <- "SpaNorm"

# Use all 480 genes as variable features; on a targeted Xenium panel every gene was chosen as informative by design,
# and FindVariableFeatures("vst") requires a counts layer the SpaNorm assay does not carry.
VariableFeatures(xen) <- rownames(xen)

# RunPCA expects a scale.data layer; SCT writes it implicitly, but our custom SpaNorm assay only carries the data layer.
xen <- ScaleData(xen, verbose = FALSE)

# Default npcs (50) is fine; we only use the first 30 downstream.
xen <- RunPCA(xen, npcs = 30, verbose = FALSE)

# 1:30 PCs and resolution 0.5 match 20260512_SpaNorm.qmd for a like-for-like comparison.
xen <- FindNeighbors(xen, reduction = "pca", dims = 1:30, verbose = FALSE)
xen <- FindClusters(xen, resolution = 0.5, algorithm = 1, verbose = FALSE)
xen <- RunUMAP(xen, reduction = "pca", dims = 1:30, verbose = FALSE)


# qs_save(xen, "data/20260512_microglia_switch_spanorm_p025.qs2")

title: SpaNorm: spatial-aware normalization
text:
PCA split by slide


Code
# Palette sized to the actual cluster count so colors stay stable if the resolution changes the number of clusters.
colorines <- scCustomize_Palette(num_groups = n_distinct(xen$seurat_clusters), ggplot_default_colors = FALSE)




Code
# Split by slide to compare against the sample.p=0.05 PCA in 20260512_SpaNorm.qmd.
PCAPlot(xen, split.by = "slide", cols = colorines)









Result. The PC1/PC2 envelope at sample.p = 0.25 reproduces the fan shape from 20260512_SpaNorm_sample005.html, with Slide1 and Slide2 occupying matched coordinate ranges (PC1 from -10 to ~15; PC2 from -8 to ~28).
Cluster identities differ between the two builds (cluster 4, green, dominates the upper PC2 region here, whereas cluster 2, red, did so at sample.p = 0.05); that is the expected Louvain re-labelling under slightly shifted PCA coordinates. The fan structure itself is stable. We read this as PCA-level stability under a five-fold larger fitting subsample.

title: SpaNorm: spatial-aware normalization
text:
UMAP


Code
# label = TRUE prints the cluster id inside each UMAP island so the markers panels below stay legible.
DimPlot(xen, cols = colorines, label = TRUE, label.size = 5) + NoLegend()









Result. sample.p = 0.25 yields 25 clusters, the same count as sample.p = 0.05 at the matched Louvain resolution.
The 2D layout re-orients (cluster 0, dark, now sits at the top of the UMAP rather than at the bottom-center), but the same set of discrete islands appears, including the small satellite clusters near the right edge of the embedding (clusters 14 and 17 at umap_1 ~ 14). Cluster count and topology are stable; the absolute UMAP layout is not, which is the expected behavior under any seeded re-embedding.

Per slide


Code
# Side-by-side UMAPs per slide; the failure mode we test against is any cluster that only exists in one slide.
DimPlot(xen, split.by = "slide", cols = colorines) + NoLegend()









Result. The two slide-imbalance regions flagged at sample.p = 0.05 (top-left island carrying different cluster identities, bottom-center lobe denser on Slide2) are much less pronounced at sample.p = 0.25.
Both slides now populate the same cluster geometry with comparable densities: the dark cluster 0 at the top, the gray/red lower-left lobe, and the right-edge satellites all replicate on both panels. The package default absorbs the slide effect more cleanly than the 5 % subsample on this dataset.
On the strength of this panel alone, we promote sample.p = 0.25 to the project default and revisit the need for Harmony only if the three-way normalization comparison still shows residual slide structure.


Per sample


Code
# Per-sample facets; 4s2M_F2 (partial section) is the sample most likely to behave differently.
DimPlot(xen, split.by = "sample_id", ncol = 3, cols = colorines) + NoLegend()









Result. All six complete sections (4s2_F1, F2, F3 and 4s2M_F1, F3, F4) reproduce the 25-cluster landscape with similar proportions.
4s2M_F2 still contributes fewer cells, as expected for the partial section, but the cells it does contribute land in the same basins as the matched complete sections, with cluster 0 (dark) at the top and the lower-left gray/red lobe both well represented. The partial-section flag stays a cell-count flag, not a normalization or cluster-identity flag.

title: SpaNorm: spatial-aware normalization
text:
Summary
Per-sample SpaNorm at sample.p = 0.25 produces the same 25-cluster decomposition as sample.p = 0.05 and better balances the two slides on the per-slide UMAP. Convergence is clean on all seven fits (see 20260513_SpaNorm_samplep025_convergence.md). We adopt sample.p = 0.25 as the project default and update the SCT-vs-SpaNorm three-way comparison to read this build’s checkpoint.

title: SpaNorm: spatial-aware normalization
text:
References

Salim A et al. “SpaNorm: spatially-aware normalization for spatial transcriptomics data.” Genome Biology, 2025. DOI: 10.1186/s13059-025-03565-y.
SpaNorm vignette: https://bhuvad.github.io/SpaNorm/articles/SpaNorm.html.

title: Microglia sub-cluster on the clean SpaNorm build
text:
The clean SpaNorm Louvain-0.5 cluster 2 (data/20260516_microglia_switch_spanorm_p025_clean.qs2) holds the entire microglia population and carries Cx3cr1 / P2ry12 / Tmem119 alongside Cst7 / Trem2 / Cd14 in its top-10, so it mixes homeostatic and DAM states in one cluster. The clean-0.7 global build splits them globally (cluster 12 homeostatic, cluster 6 DAM), but a targeted re-fit on microglia alone is the cleaner way to resolve finer microglia states (homeostatic, IRM intermediate, DAM Stage-1 Trem2-dependent, DAM Stage-2 Trem2-independent) before the E4-vs-E2 DE.
Here, we subset to cluster 2, re-fit SpaNorm per sample on the microglia counts, then re-run ScaleData / PCA(30) / FindNeighbors / Louvain / UMAP, look at PCA balance per slide, UMAP per slide and per sample, FindAllMarkers, and a fiddle violin panel; first at Louvain 0.5 and then at 0.3 to bracket the resolution from both sides, matching the structure of 20260516_SpaNorm_samplep025_clean.qmd.
Normalization is re-fit on the microglia subset. SpaNorm learns three things from the data: a per-cell library-size correction, a per-section baseline expression level, and a smooth spatial component for how expression varies across the tissue. In the whole-brain fit, the per-section baseline is dominated by the most abundant cell types (oligodendrocytes and neurons); microglia contribute too few cells to shift it.
When we subset to microglia and re-fit, all three terms are recomputed from microglia counts only. The per-section baseline now tracks microglia abundance and microglia spatial density. Variation between microglia states (DAM versus homeostatic, interferon-responsive microglia, proliferating Mki67+ cells, plaque-proximal gradients) was a tiny slice of the global variance; in the re-fit it becomes the dominant signal in the PCA. The cost is that the microglia SpaNorm matrix is no longer comparable to the whole-brain object, so between-cell-type contrasts are lost.
The trade-off is sparsity. Per-section microglia counts are ~7k for the complete sections and ~3.5k for the partial 4s2M_F2 section, which is sparse but should still support the default df.tps = 6 thin-plate splines.
Bibliography:

Keren-Shaul et al., “A unique microglia type associated with restricting development of Alzheimer’s disease,” Cell, 2017. DOI: 10.1016/j.cell.2017.05.018.
Salim et al., “SpaNorm: spatially-aware normalization for spatial transcriptomics data,” Genome Biology, 2025. DOI: 10.1186/s13059-025-03565-y.
Parent notebook: 20260516_SpaNorm_samplep025_clean.qmd.

  "4s2_F3",   "Slide2_0069080",  "control",
  "4s2M_F3",  "Slide2_0069080",  "switched",
  "4s2M_F4",  "Slide2_0069080",  "switched")

# Load the clean SpaNorm object. seurat_clusters in the saved checkpoint are the Louvain-0.5 labels;
# cluster 2 is the full microglia population (Cx3cr1 / P2ry12 / Tmem119 / Cst7 / Trem2 / Cd14).
xen <- qs_read("data/20260516_microglia_switch_spanorm_p025_clean.qs2")
mg  <- subset(xen, subset = seurat_clusters == "2")

# Drop the parent SpaNorm assay, PCA / UMAP / SNN and seurat_clusters before re-fitting SpaNorm on
# microglia only. The Xenium raw counts assay is what feeds the new per-sample SpaNorm fits.
# DefaultAssay must move to Xenium first; DietSeurat refuses to drop the currently-default assay.
DefaultAssay(mg) <- "Xenium"
mg <- DietSeurat(mg, assays = "Xenium", dimreducs = NULL, graphs = NULL)

# Literal slide -> FOV map; the merge of the two LoadXenium slides kept the FOVs as fov / fov.2.
slide_to_fov <- c(Slide1_0022474 = "fov", Slide2_0069080 = "fov.2")
fov_lookup   <- set_names(slide_to_fov[samples$slide], samples$sample_id)

# Per-sample SpaNorm; same helper structure as the parent build, only the input object changes.
spanorm_one_sample <- function(s, mg, fov_lookup) {
  # One tissue section at a time; SpaNorm's spatial smoothness assumption is per section.
  mg_s   <- subset(mg, subset = sample_id == s)
  # Centroids from the slide FOV; filter to microglia cells that survived the per-sample subset.
  coords <- GetTissueCoordinates(mg_s, image = fov_lookup[[s]]) |>
    filter(cell %in% Cells(mg_s))
  # Hand SpaNorm raw microglia counts plus the 2D centroids; ~7k cells per complete section,
  # ~3.5k for the partial 4s2M_F2 (still enough to support df.tps = 6).
  spe <- SpatialExperiment(
    assays        = list(counts = GetAssayData(mg_s, assay = "Xenium", layer = "counts")),
    spatialCoords = as.matrix(coords[, c("x", "y")]))
  # sample.p = 0.25 and df.tps = 6 held identical to the parent SpaNorm build for like-for-like comparison.
  spe <- SpaNorm(spe,
                 sample.p   = 0.25,
                 df.tps     = 6,
                 adj.method = "auto",
                 backend    = "auto",
                 verbose    = TRUE)
  out <- SummarizedExperiment::assay(spe, "logcounts")
  colnames(out) <- Cells(mg_s)
  out}

# Run SpaNorm on each of the seven samples; map keeps the per-sample matrices named for the reduce step.
norm_list <- map(samples$sample_id, spanorm_one_sample,
                 mg = mg, fov_lookup = fov_lookup) |>
  set_names(samples$sample_id)

# Bind per-sample matrices into one wide normalized matrix and reorder to the merged object's cell order.
# Qualify reduce() because SpatialExperiment loads IRanges, whose reduce() masks purrr::reduce.
norm_mat <- purrr::reduce(norm_list, cbind)
norm_mat <- norm_mat[, Cells(mg)]

# Attach as the new SpaNorm assay on the microglia object; CreateAssay5Object avoids the
# "counts layer not present" warning that CreateAssayObject (v3) triggers inside a v5 object.
mg[["SpaNorm"]] <- CreateAssay5Object(data = norm_mat)
DefaultAssay(mg) <- "SpaNorm"

# All 480 panel genes stay as variable features; the targeted panel is small enough that
# off-microglia genes contribute nothing to the microglia PCs anyway, and FindVariableFeatures("vst")
# does not work on a v5 assay that carries only the data layer.
VariableFeatures(mg) <- rownames(mg)

# A custom v5 assay carrying only the data layer needs an explicit ScaleData before RunPCA.
mg <- ScaleData(mg, verbose = FALSE)

# Default npcs = 50 is fine; we keep 30 PCs to match the parent build, even though microglia alone
# almost certainly need fewer informative PCs (most loadings will collapse to noise after PC ~10).
mg <- RunPCA(mg, npcs = 30, verbose = FALSE)

# Build the SNN graph once, then run Louvain at both resolutions inside the build.
# Each FindClusters call overwrites seurat_clusters and Idents(mg), so we stash the
# result of each call into a named metadata column to keep both label sets available
# on the saved object without having to re-run FindClusters at render time.
mg <- FindNeighbors(mg, reduction = "pca", dims = 1:30, verbose = FALSE)

mg <- FindClusters(mg, resolution = 0.5, algorithm = 1, verbose = FALSE)
mg$clusters_res05 <- mg$seurat_clusters

mg <- FindClusters(mg, resolution = 0.3, algorithm = 1, verbose = FALSE)
mg$clusters_res03 <- mg$seurat_clusters

mg <- RunUMAP(mg, reduction = "pca", dims = 1:30, verbose = FALSE)

qs_save(mg, "data/20260517_microglia_subcluster.qs2")

title: Microglia sub-cluster on the clean SpaNorm build
text:
PCA split by slide


Code
# Palette sized to the Louvain-0.5 sub-cluster count; this section uses the clusters_res05 column.
colorines <- scCustomize_Palette(num_groups = n_distinct(mg$clusters_res05), ggplot_default_colors = FALSE)




Code
# Split by slide so any slide-driven sub-structure inside microglia shows up before downstream DE.
PCAPlot(mg, group.by = "clusters_res05", split.by = "slide", cols = colorines)









Result. PC1/PC2 envelopes match across slides in shape and span, but one cluster (the oligodendrocyte-contamination cluster, see UMAP-per-slide below) sits at different density on each slide. The PCA itself is fine; the slide-difference signal lives in cluster density not in PC-coordinate range.

title: Microglia sub-cluster on the clean SpaNorm build
text:
UMAP


Code
# label = TRUE prints the sub-cluster id inside each island so the marker panels below stay legible.
DimPlot(mg, group.by = "clusters_res05", cols = colorines, label = TRUE, label.size = 8, label.color = "grey20") + NoLegend()









Result. 11 sub-clusters at Louvain 0.5. Five of them are true microglia states (0 DAM Stage-1 with Lyz2 / Cst7 / Ccl3 / Lpl; 4 DAM Stage-2 with Spp1 / Gpnmb / H2-Eb1 / H2-Ab1 / Cd74 / Itgax / Lgals3, antigen-presenting flavor; 2 homeostatic with Tmem119 / P2ry12 / Maf / Selplg; 6 low-FC homeostatic with mild Ttr contamination; 10 IRM + proliferating with Cxcl10 FC 6.83, Mki67 5.03, Ifit1 / Ifit2, Ccl2 / Ccl12) and six are doublet / segmentation-contamination clusters of neighboring cell types (1 inhibitory neuron contamination, 3 excitatory neuron contamination, 5 reactive astrocyte contamination, 7 vascular contamination, 8 oligodendrocyte contamination, 9 multi-lineage low-specificity). The high contamination fraction is consistent with microglia being a small cell-type population (~5 % of brain cells) prone to spatial overlap with denser neighbors at segmentation boundaries. The custom APOE probes rs429358-WT-228 and rs7412-ALT-226 appear inside the DAM Stage-2 (4) and low-FC homeostatic (6) clusters respectively, which is exactly where the E4-vs-E2 contrast will need to live.

Per slide


Code
# Side-by-side UMAPs per slide; failure mode is any sub-cluster that lives on only one slide.
DimPlot(mg, group.by = "clusters_res05", split.by = "slide", cols = colorines) + NoLegend()









Result. A visible slide effect at 0.5: one or more contamination clusters (most prominently the oligodendrocyte-contamination cluster 8 and the multi-lineage cluster 9) sit at clearly different densities on Slide1 vs Slide2. The five true microglia states reproduce on both slides at matched densities. This is the reason we promote the 0.3 decomposition (below) for the downstream DE: dropping resolution merges those slide-imbalanced contamination clusters into broader categories that balance across slides.


Per sample


Code
# Per-sample facets; 4s2M_F2 (partial section) still expected to contribute fewer microglia than the others.
DimPlot(mg, group.by = "clusters_res05", split.by = "sample_id", ncol = 3, cols = colorines) + NoLegend()









Result. All six complete sections (4s2_F1, F2, F3 and 4s2M_F1, F3, F4) contribute to every microglia sub-cluster. 4s2M_F2 partial-section is sparser as expected, but its microglia land in the shared basins. The slide imbalance noted above is not a single-sample effect; the contamination clusters that are slide-imbalanced are imbalanced across both slides’ samples symmetrically.

title: Microglia sub-cluster on the clean SpaNorm build
Microglia sub-cluster on the clean SpaNorm build
text:
Reclustering at lower Louvain resolution (0.3)
The 0.5 sub-clustering already produced a non-trivial microglia decomposition; here we test the opposite direction. Lowering Louvain to 0.3 on the same microglia PCA / SNN graph collapses neighboring sub-clusters and shows which sub-states are most robust. If the 0.5 split between homeostatic and DAM-like cells survives at 0.3, that boundary is the load-bearing one for the E4-vs-E2 DE; if 0.3 merges all microglia into one or two macro-clusters, the finer 0.5 sub-clusters are likely transitional or noise-driven rather than canonical Keren-Shaul (Cell, 2017) states. The 0.3 labels are already written into mg$clusters_res03 by the build chunk above, so we just re-color the plots and re-derive markers from that column.


Code
colorines_03 <- scCustomize_Palette(num_groups = n_distinct(mg$clusters_res03), ggplot_default_colors = FALSE)



UMAP


Code
DimPlot(mg, group.by = "clusters_res03", cols = colorines_03, label = TRUE, label.size = 10) + NoLegend()









Result. 7 sub-clusters at Louvain 0.3, collapsed from the 11 sub-clusters at 0.5. Three are true microglia states: cluster 0 (consolidated DAM, merging the 0.5 Stage-1 / Stage-2 pair: Spp1 / Cst7 / Gpnmb / H2-Eb1 / Lyz2 / Cd74 / Ccl3 / Lpl / Itgax / Lgals3), cluster 1 (consolidated homeostatic, merging the two 0.5 homeostatic clusters: Tmem119 / P2ry12 / Ptgs1 / Maf / Lpcat2 / Ltc4s), and cluster 6 (IRM + proliferating, unchanged from the 0.5 build’s cluster 10: Cxcl10 FC 6.83, Mki67 5.10, Ifit1 / Ifit2, Ccl2 / Ccl12, Plac8). Four are contamination clusters (2 excitatory neuron, 3 inhibitory + astrocyte merged, 4 oligodendrocyte, 5 vascular). The multi-lineage low-specificity cluster from the 0.5 build (cluster 9) dissolves entirely into its neighbors at this coarser cut. The DAM Stage-1 / Stage-2 boundary that 0.5 resolved is not load-bearing enough to survive at 0.3, but the DAM-versus-homeostatic boundary is.


Per slide


Code
DimPlot(mg, group.by = "clusters_res03", split.by = "slide", cols = colorines_03) + NoLegend()









Result. No slide effect at 0.3. Every one of the seven sub-clusters populates Slide1 and Slide2 at matched densities. The slide imbalance seen at 0.5 was driven by the finer contamination splits (oligodendrocyte-contamination cluster 8, multi-lineage cluster 9, possibly vascular cluster 7); collapsing those into broader categories at 0.3 absorbs the imbalance. This is the load-bearing reason to promote the 0.3 decomposition for the downstream E4-vs-E2 DE rather than the 0.5 decomposition.


Per sample


Code
DimPlot(mg, group.by = "clusters_res03", split.by = "sample_id", ncol = 3, cols = colorines_03) + NoLegend()









Result. All six complete sections reproduce the 7-cluster decomposition at comparable proportions; 4s2M_F2 partial-section is sparser as expected but its microglia still land in the shared basins. The three true microglia clusters (0 DAM, 1 homeostatic, 6 IRM + proliferating) and the four contamination clusters all populate every animal, so neither the DAM-versus-homeostatic split nor the IRM cluster is driven by one sample.


Find all markers (resolution 0.3)

Idents(mg) <- "clusters_res03"
microglia_markers_03 <- FindAllMarkers(mg, only.pos = TRUE, densify = TRUE)

write_csv(microglia_markers_03, file = "results/20260517-find_all_markers_microglia_subcluster_res03.csv")

title: Microglia sub-cluster on the clean SpaNorm build
text:
Summary
Re-fitting SpaNorm on the microglia subset and re-clustering at Louvain 0.5 yields 11 sub-clusters (five microglia states + six doublet / contamination clusters) with a visible slide effect on at least the oligodendrocyte-contamination cluster and the multi-lineage low-specificity cluster. Lowering Louvain to 0.3 collapses to 7 sub-clusters and removes the slide effect entirely: three true microglia states survive (0 DAM consolidated, 1 homeostatic consolidated, 6 IRM + proliferating with Cxcl10 / Mki67 / Ifit1 / Ifit2 / Ccl2 / Ccl12) alongside four contamination clusters (2 excitatory neuron, 3 inhibitory + astrocyte, 4 oligodendrocyte, 5 vascular). The DAM Stage-1 / Stage-2 split that 0.5 resolved (and the antigen-presenting H2-Eb1 / H2-Ab1 enrichment inside the 0.5 Stage-2 cluster) is not robust enough to survive the coarser 0.3 cut, but the DAM-versus-homeostatic boundary is. The custom APOE probes (rs429358-WT for E3/E4, rs7412-ALT for E2) sit inside the microglia DAM and homeostatic clusters respectively at 0.5, so the E4-vs-E2 DE can be run on cluster 0 (DAM) and cluster 1 (homeostatic) of the 0.3 decomposition with the contamination clusters (2, 3, 4, 5) excluded from the contrast. We promote the 0.3 decomposition as the working microglia scaffold; sub-cluster annotation, plaque-proximity analysis and the E4-vs-E2 pseudobulk DE start from there.

title: Microglia sub-cluster on the clean SpaNorm build
text:
References

Keren-Shaul H et al. “A unique microglia type associated with restricting development of Alzheimer’s disease.” Cell, 2017. DOI: 10.1016/j.cell.2017.05.018.
Salim A et al. “SpaNorm: spatially-aware normalization for spatial transcriptomics data.” Genome Biology, 2025. DOI: 10.1186/s13059-025-03565-y.
Parent notebook: 20260516_SpaNorm_samplep025_clean.qmd.

title: Microglia pseudobulk DE: control (E4) vs switched (E2)
text:
Differential expression on the microglia sub-cluster build (data/20260517_microglia_subcluster.qs2, Louvain 0.5, 11 sub-clusters) to compare the E4 control microglia (genotype label 4s2, condition = "control") against the E2 switched microglia (genotype label 4s2M, condition = "switched").
Pseudobulk per sample (7 sections: three 4s2 control sections, four 4s2M switched sections). Pseudobulk aggregation collapses each sub-cluster’s cells per sample into a single sample-level count vector before DESeq2. The unit of replication becomes the biological sample (n = 3 control, n = 4 switched).
Code
# Seurat v5 holds the microglia sub-cluster object with the raw Xenium counts assay we aggregate from.
library(Seurat)
library(tidyverse)
# qs2 reads the multi-GB checkpoint roughly 10x faster than saveRDS.
library(qs2)
# presto gives a faster FindMarkers backend for the non-DESeq2 paths anayet can take.
# DESeq2 itself is invoked by FindMarkers(test.use = "DESeq2").
library(DESeq2)
# EnhancedVolcano renders the volcano plot inside anayet().
library(EnhancedVolcano)
# patchwork + gridExtra combine the volcano with the significant-DEG table side by side.
library(patchwork)
library(gridExtra)
library(kableExtra)

# Source the shared helper functions; anayet() wraps FindMarkers + EnhancedVolcano for the pseudobulk DE.
source("R/20260514-helper_functions.R")

# Seed any stochastic step so the results are reproducible across renders.
set.seed(1)

# Reload the microglia sub-cluster checkpoint built by 20260517_microglia_subcluster.qmd.
mg <- qs_read("data/20260517_microglia_subcluster.qs2")

# AggregateExpression pulls from the DefaultAssay's counts layer; we want the raw Xenium counts,
# not the SpaNorm log-residuals that are the analysis-default assay on this object.
DefaultAssay(mg) <- "Xenium"

title: Microglia pseudobulk DE: control (E4) vs switched (E2)
section: Pseudobulk DE on the full microglia object
text:
Pseudobulk DE on the full microglia object

Merge every bona fide microglia sub-cluster (0, 2, 4, 6, 10) and the likely-contaminated clusters (1, 3, 5, 7, 8, 9) into a single sample-level pseudobulk contrast.

Positive log2FC means up in the E2-switched arm.


Code
# Pool the five bona fide microglia clusters; drop the contamination clusters so the per-sample pseudobulks
# are not contaminated by neuronal / astrocyte / OL / vascular transcripts segmented into microglia masks.
# mg_pool <- subset(mg, clusters_res05 %in% c("0", "2", "4", "6", "10"))


# Uses the full microglia object
mg_pool <- mg

# Aggregate raw Xenium counts per sample; the resulting Seurat object has one column per biological sample.
bulk_pool <- AggregateExpression(mg_pool,
                                 assays        = "Xenium",
                                 group.by      = "sample_id",
                                 return.seurat = TRUE)

# AggregateExpression rewrites sample IDs ("4s2_F1" -> "g4s2-F1"); rebuild the lookup so condition attaches.
sample_meta_pool <- mg_pool@meta.data |>
  distinct(sample_id, condition) |>
  mutate(bulk_id = paste0("g", gsub("_", "-", sample_id)))

bulk_pool$condition <- sample_meta_pool$condition[match(colnames(bulk_pool), sample_meta_pool$bulk_id)]
stopifnot(!any(is.na(bulk_pool$condition)))
Idents(bulk_pool) <- "condition"

# Run DESeq2 through anayet() with the same thresholds as the per-state loop, for like-for-like comparison.
res_pool <- anayet(bulk_pool,
                   ident1          = "switched",
                   ident2          = "control",
                   test_method     = "DESeq2",
                   p_adj_threshold = 0.1,
                   min_pct         = 0.3,
                   plot_title      = "Pseudobulk DE (full microglia pool)",
                   print_plot      = FALSE)

# Always label the 5 lowest-p genes (blue) plus any FDR < 0.1 hits (red), mirroring the per-state plots so
# the volcano stays readable even when no gene clears FDR.
top5_p_pool    <- res_pool$all_results |> as_tibble(rownames = "gene") |> slice_min(p_val, n = 5, with_ties = FALSE) |> pull(gene)
sig_genes_pool <- rownames(res_pool$significant_results)
lab_genes_pool <- union(sig_genes_pool, top5_p_pool)

keyvals_pool <- rep("grey60", nrow(res_pool$all_results))
names(keyvals_pool) <- rownames(res_pool$all_results)
keyvals_pool[top5_p_pool]    <- "royalblue"
keyvals_pool[sig_genes_pool] <- "red2"

fc_range_pool <- range(res_pool$all_results$avg_log2FC, na.rm = TRUE)
y_max_pool    <- max(-log10(res_pool$all_results$p_val), na.rm = TRUE)

res_pool$volcano_plot <- EnhancedVolcano(res_pool$all_results,
                                         lab            = rownames(res_pool$all_results),
                                         selectLab      = lab_genes_pool,
                                         x              = "avg_log2FC",
                                         y              = "p_val",
                                         title          = "Pseudobulk DE (full microglia)",
                                         subtitle       = "switched (E2) vs. control (E4)",
                                         caption        = "BLUE: 5 lowest-p genes (FDR>0.1)\nRED: FDR < 0.1, min_pct = 0.3",
                                         labFace        = "italic",
                                         pCutoff        = 1,
                                         FCcutoff       = 0.001,
                                         colCustom      = keyvals_pool,
                                         legendPosition = "none",
                                         drawConnectors = TRUE,
                                         directionConnectors = "both",
                                         max.overlaps   = Inf,
                                         xlim           = c(floor(fc_range_pool[1]), ceiling(fc_range_pool[2])),
                                         ylim           = c(0, max(ceiling(y_max_pool * 1.05), 1)))

# Fall back to the 5 lowest-p genes when no gene clears FDR < 0.1, so the side table is never empty.
if (nrow(res_pool$significant_results) > 0) {
  table_pool <- res_pool$significant_results |>
    as_tibble(rownames = "gene") |>
    select(gene, p_val, avg_log2FC, p_val_adj) |>
    mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) signif(x, 3))) |>
    arrange(p_val_adj)
} else {
  table_pool <- res_pool$all_results |>
    as_tibble(rownames = "gene") |>
    slice_min(p_val, n = 5, with_ties = FALSE) |>
    select(gene, p_val, avg_log2FC, p_val_adj) |>
    mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) signif(x, 3)))
}
table_grob_pool <- tableGrob(table_pool, theme = ttheme_minimal(base_size = 12), rows = NULL)

# 3:4 width ratio matches the per-state combined panels.
res_pool$volcano_plot + wrap_elements(table_grob_pool) +
  plot_layout(widths = c(3, 4))









No gene clears FDR < 0.1 on the full microglia object.
The five lowest-p genes split into two readings. The expected biology comes from Ccl5 (+0.91, p = 4.3e-3), Ifit1 (+0.76, p = 1.0e-2), and Cxcl10 (+0.88, p = 1.9e-2). All three carry positive log2FC and all three are Type-I IFN / inflammatory chemokines.
The other two top hits are likely segmentation contamination. Plcb4 (-0.56, p = 1.3e-2) is a phospholipase C beta isoform expressed in cerebellar Purkinje cells and cortical projection neurons, not in microglia. Tunar (-0.40, p = 1.7e-2) is a long non-coding RNA enriched in neuronal lineages and absent from canonical microglia signatures.

title: Microglia pseudobulk DE: control (E4) vs switched (E2)
text:
Pseudobulk DE per microglia state
Subclusters considered:

Cluster 0: DAM Stage-1, Trem2-dependent core (Lyz2 / Ccl3 / Ccl4 / Cst7 / Clec7a / Ctsb / Lpl). 6,919 cells.
Cluster 4: DAM Stage-2, antigen-presenting late-stage (Spp1 / Apoc4 / Gpnmb / H2-Eb1 / Lgals3 / H2-Ab1 / Cd74 / Itgax). 2,962 cells. Carries the custom APOE rs429358-WT-228 probe (E2 allele, T at codon 112) in its top-10.
Cluster 2: Canonical homeostatic microglia (Tmem119 / P2ry12 / Maf / Ltc4s / Pnp / Htra1 / Selplg). 5,123 cells.
Cluster 6: Low-FC homeostatic boundary / CP-adjacent subset (Tmem119 / Cd68 / Ptgs1 / Gpr34 / P2ry12 / Mertk). 2,082 cells. Carries the custom APOE rs7412-ALT-226:T probe (E2 allele) in its top-10.
Cluster 10: IRM + proliferating microglia (Cxcl10 / Mki67 / Ccl2 / Ccl12 / Ifit1 / Ifit2 / Ifitm3 / Plac8). only 399 cells.

The other six likely contaminated clusters (1 inhib + glut neuron, 3 cortical exc neuron, 5 reactive astrocyte, 7 vascular, 8 oligodendrocyte, 9 multi-lineage doublet pool) are excluded from this DE.

Code
# Map each microglia state to the cluster id it covers.
microglia_states <- list(
  "DAM Stage-1 (cluster 0)"                     = "0",
  "DAM Stage-2 (cluster 4)"                     = "4",
  "Homeostatic canonical (cluster 2)"           = "2",
  "Homeostatic low-FC / CP-adjacent (cluster 6)" = "6",
  "IRM + proliferating (cluster 10)"            = "10")

# Per-cluster interpretive commentary, keyed by cluster id and emitted under each volcano. Each block
# splits the top-5 hits into expected microglial biology and likely segmentation contamination. Numbers
# match the per-state DESeq2 tables; keep this in sync with the rendered Results section above.
cluster_commentary <- list(
  "0" = paste(
    "All five top-5 hits carry positive log2FC. `Lgals3bp` (+0.49),",
    "`Ccl12` (+0.67), `Ifit2` (+0.39), and `Cd74` (+0.42) are bona fide microglial",
    "inflammatory / IFN / antigen-presenting genes; the fifth hit is the E2-allele",
    "APOE probe `rs7412-ALT-226:T` (+0.97). Expected biology dominates: the E2-switched",
    "group shows a coordinated IFN / DAM activation signature plus detectable E2 transcript.",
    "No neuronal, astrocytic, or OL marker enters the top-5, so I read this cluster as",
    "the cleanest of the five; the contamination hypothesis is not needed to explain any",
    "of the labelled hits."),
  "4" = paste(
    "Expected biology comes from `Lgals3bp` (+0.51), `Ccl12` (+0.87), and the E2",
    "probe `rs7412-ALT-226:T` (+1.03), reproducing the cluster-0 pattern. Likely",
    "contamination comes from `Cnr1` (+1.04) and `Slc17a7` (+0.44). `Cnr1` is the",
    "cannabinoid receptor 1 and `Slc17a7` is VGLUT1; both are neuronal markers, not",
    "microglial. DAM Stage-2 cells sit near amyloid plaques where neuronal processes"),
  "2" = paste(
    "Expected biology dominates. `Tmem119` (-0.37) declines in the E2-switched arm",
    "(homeostatic marker) and `Csf1` (+0.34) rises. Both custom APOE probes",
    "hybridize to T alleles and therefore both detect the E2 transcript in this model; both rise",
    " in the switched arm: `rs429358-WT-228` (+0.94, p = 3.7e-3; T at codon 112) and",
    " `rs7412-ALT-226:T` (+0.70, p = 1.9e-2; T at codon 158). The single likely contamination hit is",
    "`Pdgfra` (-0.40), an oligodendrocyte-progenitor marker, not microglial;",
    "its appearance fits segmentation bleed from OPC neighbors."),
  "6" = paste(
    "Expected biology comes from `Tmem119` (-0.47) declining and `Lgals3bp` (+0.62)",
    "rising in the E2-switched arm, plus the E2-allele probe",
    "`rs429358-WT-228` (+1.58, p = 1.6e-2; T at codon 112) becoming detectable.",
    "Likely contamination comes from `Ms4a4b` (+1.02), a T-cell / lymphocyte marker",
    "not microglial, and `Gstm1` (-0.64), broadly expressed across cell types."),
  "10" = paste(
    "The only cluster that clears FDR. `Ccl12` (+1.32, FDR 3.0e-5),",
    "`Lgals3bp` (+0.86, FDR 5.9e-3), and `Ifit2` (+1.91, FDR 9.2e-2) line up with",
    "the IFN / inflammatory shift seen across the other states; `Mki67` (-1.00,",
    "FDR 9.6e-3) drops, so the switched arm shifts away from proliferation. The four",
    "FDR hits read as expected biology. `rs429358-WT-228` (+1.86, p = 6.2e-4) appears",
    "as the fifth lowest-p but does not clear FDR; the E2-allele probe (T at codon 112)",
    "rising in the switched arm is consistent with E2 transcript becoming detectable."))

de_results <- imap(microglia_states, \(cluster_ids, label) {
  # Subset to the cells of one microglia state; %in% accepts both single ids and bound id-vectors.
  sub <- subset(mg, clusters_res05 %in% cluster_ids)

  # Aggregate raw Xenium counts per sample; return.seurat = TRUE produces a sample-level Seurat object.
  bulk <- AggregateExpression(sub,
                              assays        = "Xenium",
                              group.by      = "sample_id",
                              return.seurat = TRUE)

  # AggregateExpression silently replaces underscores in sample IDs with hyphens, and prepends "g" to any
  # group label that starts with a digit ("starts with a number, appending `g` to ensure valid variable names").
  # Our sample IDs (4s2_F1, 4s2M_F1, ...) hit both rules, so we mirror them when matching the per-sample
  # condition labels back to the pseudobulk column names.
  sample_meta <- sub@meta.data |>
    distinct(sample_id, condition) |>
    mutate(bulk_id = paste0("g", gsub("_", "-", sample_id)))

  bulk$condition <- sample_meta$condition[match(colnames(bulk), sample_meta$bulk_id)]
  # Hard-stop if the metadata join produced NA; common cause is forgetting the underscore -> hyphen fix.
  stopifnot(!any(is.na(bulk$condition)))
  Idents(bulk) <- "condition"

  # Run the DE through anayet()
  # plot_title is left short because anayet() appends " in <cell_type>" derived from ident1; we rebuild the volcano below.
  res <- anayet(bulk,
                ident1          = "switched",
                ident2          = "control",
                test_method     = "DESeq2",
                p_adj_threshold = 0.1,
                min_pct         = 0.3,
                plot_title      = "Pseudobulk DE",
                print_plot      = FALSE)

  # Rebuild the volcano so the 5 genes with the lowest raw p-value are always labelled, even when the
  # FDR-significant set is empty (most p_val_adj sit at 1 in low-power pseudobulks on a 480-gene panel).
  # Significant-at-FDR-0.1 genes stay in red; the top-5-by-p-value sit in blue if they are not already significant.
  top5_p   <- res$all_results |> as_tibble(rownames = "gene") |> slice_min(p_val, n = 5, with_ties = FALSE) |> pull(gene)
  sig_genes <- rownames(res$significant_results)
  lab_genes <- union(sig_genes, top5_p)

  keyvals <- rep("grey60", nrow(res$all_results))
  names(keyvals) <- rownames(res$all_results)
  keyvals[top5_p]   <- "royalblue"
  keyvals[sig_genes] <- "red2"

  fc_range  <- range(res$all_results$avg_log2FC, na.rm = TRUE)
  y_max     <- max(-log10(res$all_results$p_val), na.rm = TRUE)

  res$volcano_plot <- EnhancedVolcano(res$all_results,
                                      lab            = rownames(res$all_results),
                                      selectLab      = lab_genes,
                                      x              = "avg_log2FC",
                                      y              = "p_val",
                                      title          = paste("Pseudobulk DE in", label),
                                      subtitle       = "switched (E2) vs. control (E4)",
                                      caption        = "BLUE: 5 lowest-p genes (FDR>0.1)\nRED: FDR < 0.1, min_pct = 0.3",
                                      labFace         = "italic",
                                      pCutoff        = 1,
                                      FCcutoff       = 0.001,
                                      colCustom      = keyvals,
                                      legendPosition = "none",
                                      drawConnectors = TRUE,
                                      directionConnectors = "both",
                                      max.overlaps   = Inf,
                                      xlim           = c(floor(fc_range[1]), ceiling(fc_range[2])),
                                      ylim           = c(0, max(ceiling(y_max * 1.05), 1)))

  # Emit a QMD section header so the rendered report is navigable.
  cat("\n\n### ", label, "\n\n", sep = "")
  cat("Pseudobulk DESeq2 on", ncol(bulk), "samples (", sum(bulk$condition == "control"),
      "control vs", sum(bulk$condition == "switched"), "switched).",
      "Total genes tested:", res$summary$total_genes,
      "| Significant at FDR < 0.05:", res$summary$significant_genes, "\n\n")

  # Build the table grob. When FDR-significant hits exist, show them. Otherwise fall back to the 5 genes
  # with the lowest raw p-value so the table mirrors the volcano labels and the user can still read the
  # top of the ranked list even when no gene clears FDR < 0.1.
  if (nrow(res$significant_results) > 0) {
    sig <- res$significant_results |>
      as_tibble(rownames = "gene") |>
      select(gene, p_val, avg_log2FC, p_val_adj) |>
      mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) signif(x, 3))) |>
      arrange(p_val_adj)
    table_grob <- tableGrob(sig, theme = ttheme_minimal(base_size = 12), rows = NULL)
  } else {
    top5 <- res$all_results |>
      as_tibble(rownames = "gene") |>
      slice_min(p_val, n = 5, with_ties = FALSE) |>
      select(gene, p_val, avg_log2FC, p_val_adj) |>
      mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) signif(x, 3)))
    table_grob <- tableGrob(top5, theme = ttheme_minimal(base_size = 12), rows = NULL)
  }

  # Combine the volcano with the significant-DEG table side by side; 2:5 width ratio mirrors the skill default
  # adjusted for the wider table we want here.
  combined <- res$volcano_plot + wrap_elements(table_grob) +
    plot_layout(widths = c(3, 4))
  print(combined)

  # Emit the per-cluster expected-biology vs contamination commentary below the panel; results: asis renders
  # the cat'd markdown verbatim.
  cat("\n\n", cluster_commentary[[cluster_ids]], "\n\n", sep = "")

  res
})


DAM Stage-1 (cluster 0)
Pseudobulk DESeq2 on 7 samples ( 3 control vs 4 switched). Total genes tested: 480 | Significant at FDR < 0.05: 0

All five top-5 hits carry positive log2FC. Lgals3bp (+0.49), Ccl12 (+0.67), Ifit2 (+0.39), and Cd74 (+0.42) are bona fide microglial inflammatory / IFN / antigen-presenting genes; the fifth hit is the E2-allele APOE probe rs7412-ALT-226:T (+0.97). Expected biology dominates: the E2-switched group shows a coordinated IFN / DAM activation signature plus detectable E2 transcript. No neuronal, astrocytic, or OL marker enters the top-5, so I read this cluster as the cleanest of the five; the contamination hypothesis is not needed to explain any of the labelled hits.


DAM Stage-2 (cluster 4)
Pseudobulk DESeq2 on 7 samples ( 3 control vs 4 switched). Total genes tested: 480 | Significant at FDR < 0.05: 0

Expected biology comes from Lgals3bp (+0.51), Ccl12 (+0.87), and the E2 probe rs7412-ALT-226:T (+1.03), reproducing the cluster-0 pattern. Likely contamination comes from Cnr1 (+1.04) and Slc17a7 (+0.44). Cnr1 is the cannabinoid receptor 1 and Slc17a7 is VGLUT1; both are neuronal markers, not microglial. DAM Stage-2 cells sit near amyloid plaques where neuronal processes


Homeostatic canonical (cluster 2)
Pseudobulk DESeq2 on 7 samples ( 3 control vs 4 switched). Total genes tested: 480 | Significant at FDR < 0.05: 0

Expected biology dominates. Tmem119 (-0.37) declines in the E2-switched arm (homeostatic marker) and Csf1 (+0.34) rises. Both custom APOE probes hybridize to T alleles and therefore both detect the E2 transcript in this model; both rise in the switched arm: rs429358-WT-228 (+0.94, p = 3.7e-3; T at codon 112) and rs7412-ALT-226:T (+0.70, p = 1.9e-2; T at codon 158). The single likely contamination hit is Pdgfra (-0.40), an oligodendrocyte-progenitor marker, not microglial; its appearance fits segmentation bleed from OPC neighbors.


Homeostatic low-FC / CP-adjacent (cluster 6)
Pseudobulk DESeq2 on 7 samples ( 3 control vs 4 switched). Total genes tested: 477 | Significant at FDR < 0.05: 0

Expected biology comes from Tmem119 (-0.47) declining and Lgals3bp (+0.62) rising in the E2-switched arm, plus the E2-allele probe rs429358-WT-228 (+1.58, p = 1.6e-2; T at codon 112) becoming detectable. Likely contamination comes from Ms4a4b (+1.02), a T-cell / lymphocyte marker not microglial, and Gstm1 (-0.64), broadly expressed across cell types.


IRM + proliferating (cluster 10)
Pseudobulk DESeq2 on 7 samples ( 3 control vs 4 switched). Total genes tested: 477 | Significant at FDR < 0.05: 4

The only cluster that clears FDR. Ccl12 (+1.32, FDR 3.0e-5), Lgals3bp (+0.86, FDR 5.9e-3), and Ifit2 (+1.91, FDR 9.2e-2) line up with the IFN / inflammatory shift seen across the other states; Mki67 (-1.00, FDR 9.6e-3) drops, so the switched arm shifts away from proliferation. The four FDR hits read as expected biology. rs429358-WT-228 (+1.86, p = 6.2e-4) appears as the fifth lowest-p but does not clear FDR; the E2-allele probe (T at codon 112) rising in the switched arm is consistent with E2 transcript becoming detectable.


Code
# Persist the pooled-microglia DESeq2 table alongside the per-state tables.
res_pool$all_results |>
  as_tibble(rownames = "gene") |>
  write_csv("results/20260518-pseudobulk_DE_microglia_full_pool_switched_vs_control.csv")

# Persist the full DESeq2 result tables (all genes, not just significant) per microglia state.
# Filename slug pulls the cluster id(s) from the label (e.g. "clusters 2 + 6" -> "2_6", "cluster 0" -> "0").
iwalk(de_results, \(res, label) {
  slug <- label |>
    str_extract("clusters? [\\d \\+]+") |>
    str_remove("clusters? ") |>
    str_replace_all(" \\+ ", "_") |>
    str_trim()
  res$all_results |>
    as_tibble(rownames = "gene") |>
    write_csv(paste0("results/20260518-pseudobulk_DE_microglia_cluster", slug, "_switched_vs_control.csv"))
})

title: Microglia pseudobulk DE: control (E4) vs switched (E2)
text:
Results
Pseudobulk DESeq2 at p_adj_threshold = 0.1 and min_pct = 0.3, contrasting switched (E2, 4s2M, n = 4 samples) vs control (E4, 4s2, n = 3 samples). Positive log2FC = up in the switched arm. Genes tested per state: 480 in clusters 0, 2, 4; 477 in clusters 6 and 10 (three genes were dropped at the min_pct = 0.3 filter on the two smallest pools).
FDR-significant DEGs (p_val_adj < 0.1). Only the IRM + proliferating cluster (10, 399 cells) returns FDR-significant hits. The four other microglia states (DAM Stage-1, DAM Stage-2, homeostatic canonical, homeostatic low-FC) return zero genes at FDR < 0.1. Per-state significant-hit counts:

Top-5-by-raw-p-value across all states. Although only cluster 10 clears FDR, the top-5-by-p genes per state (labelled blue on each volcano) share a recurrent signature: Ccl12, Lgals3bp, and Ifit2 appear in the top-5 of three or four of the five states and always with positive log2FC, consistent with a coordinated IFN / inflammatory shift in the switched arm at sub-FDR levels. Tmem119 (homeostatic marker) appears in the top-5 of both homeostatic states (2 and 6) with negative log2FC.
APOE allele probes. Both custom probes on the panel hybridize to the T allele of their respective SNP and therefore both detect the E2 transcript in this model:

rs429358-WT-228: T allele at codon 112 (Cys112). The model has no E3, so the T allele uniquely marks E2.
rs7412-ALT-226:T: T allele at codon 158 (Cys158). Defines E2.

Likely segmentation contamination in non-APOE hits. Several top-5 lowest-p genes across states are not microglial: Cnr1 and Slc17a7 (excitatory neurons) in cluster 4; Pdgfra (OPC) in cluster 2; Ms4a4b (T cells) in cluster 6; Plcb4 and Tunar (neurons) in the full microglia pool. Xenium boundary-stain segmentation bleeds transcripts across adjacent cells, especially at boundaries with high transcript density, and DAM microglia near amyloid plaques carry more high-transcript neuronal neighbors than homeostatic microglia. The cluster-6 boundary leaks (Ttr / Ermn / Apod in its top-10 per the assignment note) already flag this mechanism. A per-cell AddModuleScore() filter against the seven contamination-marker sets is the proper next step before treating any of these non-microglial hits as biology.

title: Microglia pseudobulk DE: control (E4) vs switched (E2)
text:
Caveats

Cluster 10 (IRM + proliferating) is sparse on three Slide1 samples. 4s2_F1 contributes 11 cells, 4s2M_F1 contributes 23 cells, and 4s2M_F2 contributes 23 cells; the per-sample pseudobulk counts in those three replicates will be low and DESeq2 dispersion estimates may be unstable. 4s2M_F3 carries 139 of 399 cluster-10 cells (35 %), so a single sample drives a disproportionate fraction of the switched-arm signal.
lack of statistical power. Pseudobulk DE on a 480-gene panel with n = 3 vs 4 samples is underpowered. FDR adjustment over 480 tests is stringent and the seven samples are at the lower limit DESeq2 can fit dispersion against. Only cluster 10 (with the strongest IRM / proliferation contrast) produces FDR-significant hits, and three of its four DEGs survive Benjamini-Hochberg only because their p-values are extreme (≤ 2e-5).
